title: Autonomous CrazyFlie Drone Racing
text:
Software-in-the-loop simulation of gate-traversal drone racing using CrazySim, Gazebo, and ROS2


Yafu Xiao, Siyuan Gong, Anay Koorapaty


University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, ECE 484


 Code

title: Autonomous CrazyFlie Drone Racing
text:
Abstract

This project develops an autonomous flight stack for the CrazyFlie 2.1 nano quadrotor targeting gate-traversal drone racing, using a Software-in-the-Loop (SITL) simulation environment. We build on CrazySim — a Gazebo Garden-based simulator that runs the real CrazyFlie firmware in simulation — combined with Crazyswarm2 as the ROS 2 interface layer, enabling direct portability between simulation and hardware.
The simulator provides three racing tracks (Circle, Lemniscate/figure-8, and U-Turn), each populated with physical drone gates. Our current baseline is a waypoint-following controller that navigates the drone through pre-defined gate coordinates. Going forward, we plan to develop a more capable controller — incorporating pose feedback and [planned approach, e.g., model predictive control / vision-based gate detection] — to achieve faster and more robust autonomous gate traversal. All development is done in ROS 2 Humble on Ubuntu 22.04.

title: Autonomous CrazyFlie Drone Racing
text:
Approach


Problem Statement
Given a pre-defined racing track with physical gates and a knowledge of waypoints that could lead the drone to cross the the gates and the knowledge of the world coordnate of the drone itself through motion capture, command a CrazyFlie 2.1 to autonomously take off, fly through every gate in order, and land — with no human intervention.


Simulation Environment
We use CrazySim (ICRA 2024) as our SITL platform. It compiles the real CrazyFlie firmware to run on a desktop, while Gazebo Garden provides physics and sensor simulation. The simulated drone communicates over UDP with crazyflie-lib-python (cflib), and Crazyswarm2 provides a ROS 2 abstraction layer exposing standard services (Takeoff, Land, GoTo) and topics (/cf_1/pose).


Sensors / Information Used








Signal
Source
Topic / Interface




6-DOF pose (position + orientation)
Gazebo motion-capture plugin
/cf_1/pose (PoseStamped)


Gate positions
Known from track SDF world files
Hard-coded / map



The drone currently relies entirely on pose feedback from the simulated motion-capture system. No onboard camera or LIDAR is used at this stage.


Controller Architecture
The current baseline is a waypoint follower implemented as a ROS 2 node (WaypointControllerNode). It operates as a state machine:
INIT → TAKEOFF → NAVIGATE (waypoint-by-waypoint) → LAND → DONE
At each step the node calls the Crazyswarm2 GoTo service, which handles low-level position control, altitude hold, and trajectory smoothing inside the CrazyFlie firmware. Waypoint arrival is detected when the XY distance to the target drops below 0.3 m. +—————————–+ | Waypoints / Trajectory | +—————————–+ │ ▼ +—————————–+ | Outer-loop Controller | | (PID or MPC) | | Computes: | | φ_comm, θ_comm, ψ_comm, u_T | +—————————–+ │ ▼ +—————————–+ | Inner-loop PID (Firmware) | | Stabilizes angles and thrust| | Outputs motor commands | +—————————–+ │ ▼ +———–+ | Motors | +———–+ │ ▼ +———–+ | Drone | | Motion | +———–+ │ ▼ +—————————–+ | Sensors & State Estimation | | (IMU, Motion Capture, Flow) | +—————————–+ │ └─────────────► Feedback to Outer-loop Controller


Block Diagram
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        ROS 2 Node                               │
│  ┌──────────────┐    GoTo/Takeoff/Land     ┌─────────────────┐  │
│  │  Waypoint    │ ───────────────────────▶│  Crazyswarm2    │  │
│  │  Controller  │                          │  (cflib backend)│  │
│  │              │◀────────────────────────│                 │  │
│  └──────────────┘    /cf_1/pose            └────────┬────────┘  │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────┘
                                                    │ UDP (19850)
                                         ┌──────────▼──────────────┐
                                         │   CrazySim SITL         │
                                         │  (CrazyFlie firmware    │
                                         │   + Gazebo Garden)      │
                                         └─────────────────────────┘


Planned Extensions

Quantization of robustness and stability analysis through measuring lap time and gate-miss rate as quantitative metrics
Set waypoints for the other two tracks, U-Turn and Lemniscate tracks.
Implement a controller beyond the one beyond firmware burnt-in one. Create fine-grained controller aside from just going to the next waypoint in a rough and sparse set of waypoints with constant speed. We would want to have this project working just like MP2, but in SO(3). Test and compare between different longitudial and lateral controlling methods and compare the achievalbe speed of each of them through profilling the drone’s internal sensors across all three tracks.
Advanced non-linear longitudial control of speed concerning SO(3) parameters
Advanced lateral control concerning rotation with repsect to all three rotation matrices.
Advanced part by part trojectory fitting by modeling the physically optimal trajectories that the drone could fly in highest speed and try to fit these partterns into the real world waypoints to achieve this speed
If camera available: CV based pixel to world coordinates regression and then get rid of the knowledge of the knowledge on the current world coordinates of the drone(I.e., get rid of the motion capture system), but since we do not have access to any cameras, this is just a proposal

title: Autonomous CrazyFlie Drone Racing
text:
Related Work

Here are some related works in this area:

Crazyswarm2 Code Repository provides a ROS2-based framework for controlling and coordinating Crazyflie quadrotor swarms.
Related Paper 1 proposes a geometric control method for stable trajectory tracking of quadrotor UAVs.
Related Paper 2 introduces a learning-based controller that models aerodynamic effects to improve drone landing stability.

Check out this survey for a comprehensive overview of quadrotor control methods.
